title: Hasselback potatoes
subtitle: Fan-cut, herb-buttered, golden at the edges
excerpt: Potatoes cut almost through in thin, even slices, then roasted in herb butter until the edges fan out and crisp while the centres stay floury and soft. A proper Sunday-lunch potato that takes little more effort than a standard roast.
sourceNotes: The Hasselback potato originates at Hasselbacken restaurant in Stockholm, Sweden, where it was first served in the 1950s. The technique was documented in Swedish and international cookery writing from the 1960s onwards and became a standard British Sunday-roast variant in the late twentieth century. Method synthesised from the original Swedish technique.

content.text: The knife trick is this: lay a wooden spoon on either side of the potato before you cut. The spoon handles stop the blade before it cuts all the way through, leaving the potato intact at the base while the top opens into a fan as it roasts. Choose Maris Piper or King Edward potatoes of roughly equal size, and aim for slices about 3 mm apart.

The butter and oil mixture poured over the top runs down between the slices during the first part of the roast. In the second half of cooking, when the slices have begun to separate, baste two or three times so the edges colour evenly and the herb flavour penetrates the whole potato rather than sitting on the surface.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper or King Edward, roughly 200 g each, scrubbed

content.text: Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Place a potato on the chopping board with a wooden spoon on either side. Cut down through the potato in 3 mm slices, stopping when the blade meets the spoon handles. The base of the potato should remain intact. Repeat with all {{potato}} potatoes.

Mix {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, and the thyme leaves (and rosemary if using). Season with salt. Place the potatoes cut-side up in a roasting tin and spoon about half the herb butter over them, working it between the slices. Roast for 30 minutes.

After 30 minutes the slices should be beginning to fan open. Baste with the remaining herb butter, spooning it between the slices. Roast for a further 25 to 30 minutes, basting once more, until the edges are crisp and golden and the centres are soft when pierced with a skewer. Finish with sea salt flakes.

items.symptom: Potato cut all the way through
items.cause: Spoon handles did not stop the blade, or blade angled sideways
items.fix: Keep the spoon handles firmly on either side and angle the blade straight down. Even if the potato splits, it will still roast well.

items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or potatoes are too close together in the tin
items.fix: Increase to 220°C for the final 15 minutes, and make sure the potatoes are spaced with room for air to circulate.

items.cause: Potatoes were very large or the variety was a waxy type
items.fix: Cover loosely with foil and continue for 15 more minutes, then uncover to crisp.

items.symptom: Butter pooling at the base and burning
items.cause: Too much butter relative to the tin size
items.fix: Add a splash of water to the tin base and tilt to distribute. Use a tin just large enough for the potatoes.

content.text: The Hasselback potato was created in the early 1950s at Hasselbacken, a restaurant on the island of Djurgården in Stockholm, where it appeared on the menu as a Sunday special. The name stuck to the dish as it spread through Scandinavian home cooking and eventually into British cookery writing in the 1970s and 1980s. It occupies a middle ground between a roast potato and a baked potato: the interior stays floury and soft while the fanned edges become properly crisp, a combination that a standard roast potato cannot achieve.
